title: Disability Insurance in Canada
seo_title: Disability Insurance Canada | Income Protection Plans | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Compare disability insurance plans in Canada. Protect your monthly income if illness or injury stops you from working. Ontario-licensed advisors. Free quotes.

_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — motivate self-employed and unprotected workers to compare DI quotes
_meta.page_role: Product pillar page for Disability Insurance — links to CI vs Disability comparison page
_meta.writer_focus: The core message: your ability to earn income is your biggest asset. If you can't work, disability insurance replaces your paycheque. Canadians underestimate the disability risk — 1 in 3 workers will be disabled for 3+ months before age 65.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'disability insurance' 3–4 times per 500 words. Vary with 'income protection' and 'DI coverage'.
natural_variations: if you couldn't work for 6 months
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: David, 39, self-employed contractor in Mississauga — herniated disc, off work 14 months, disability insurance replaced 70% of his $8,500/month income throughout recovery
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not write 'disability insurance canada' as a stiff exact phrase. Write naturally: 'disability insurance in Canada' or 'Canadian disability coverage'.

page_data.heading: Disability Insurance: Protect Your Paycheque If You Can't Work
page_data.subheading: Your income is your most valuable asset. Disability insurance replaces up to 70–85% of your monthly income if illness or injury keeps you off work.

_writer_notes.focus: Lead with income protection framing — not the risk, the solution. 'Protect your paycheque' is the hook.
key_points: Up to 85% of pre-disability income replaced
Covers illness AND injury — not just workplace accidents
Benefits paid monthly — like a paycheque from your insurer

stats.label: Canadian workers will be off work 3+ months before age 65
Average length of a long-term disability claim in Canada
Maximum income replacement most DI plans offer

_writer_notes.focus: Stats from CLHIA (Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association) and Sun Life disability study. These are credible industry sources — cite loosely if needed.
Define DI clearly. It's monthly income replacement while you cannot work. Two main types: short-term (STD) and long-term (LTD). Personal vs group plans.
key_points: Pays a monthly benefit (not lump sum like CI) while you're unable to work
Covers both physical disabilities (back injury, accident) and mental health (depression, anxiety)
Short-term DI: pays for 3–6 months, lower elimination period (days)
Long-term DI: pays for 2 years, 5 years, or to age 65
Group DI: through employer — often insufficient, not portable
Individual DI: your own policy — stronger definitions, portable
_writer_notes.structure: Start with the scenario: 'You earn $7,000/month. You slip on ice and break your hip. You're off work for 8 months. What pays your mortgage?' Then explain DI as the answer.

page_data.heading: Own-Occupation vs Any-Occupation: The Critical Distinction

_writer_notes.focus: This is the most important concept in DI. Own-occ pays if you can't do YOUR job. Any-occ only pays if you can't do ANY job. Professionals need own-occ.
key_points: Own-occupation: you qualify if you can't perform the duties of YOUR specific occupation
Any-occupation: you only qualify if you can't work in ANY job for which you're reasonably suited
Example: surgeon loses use of right hand → own-occ pays even if they could work as a teacher
Group plans usually use any-occ definition after 2 years — individual plans can be own-occ to age 65
Own-occ is more expensive but far stronger protection
_writer_notes.real_example: Dr. Patel, dentist, develops severe hand tremors. Under own-occ: qualifies immediately — can't do dental work. Under any-occ definition: insurer may argue she can teach, consult, or work in another field — claim denied.
_writer_notes.structure: Side-by-side comparison table: Own-Occ vs Any-Occ (definition, who qualifies, cost, best for). Then plain-language explanation.

_writer_notes.focus: Explain elimination period (waiting period before benefits start). Common choices: 30, 60, 90, 120 days. Longer EP = lower premium.
key_points: Most people choose 90-day EP to balance cost vs. coverage
If you have 3 months emergency savings, 90-day EP is usually appropriate
30-day EP adds 25–35% to premium cost
Self-employed with irregular cash flow may prefer shorter EP
_writer_notes.structure: Quick table: EP length vs. cost impact. Recommendation: 90 days for most Canadians.

_writer_notes.focus: Self-employed is the #1 target. Also: employees with weak group DI, high earners, sole income providers.
key_points: Self-employed: no employer plan, no sick pay, no EI for extended illness
Employees with group DI: most group plans replace only 60% to 2 years — often not enough
High-income earners: group plans cap benefits — individual tops up
Single income households: one earner's disability is devastating
Professionals (doctors, dentists, lawyers): need own-occ definition
_writer_notes.callout_after: Note: EI sickness benefits pay 55% of earnings for up to 26 weeks. That covers short gaps — not long-term disability.

page_data.heading: How Much Does Disability Insurance Cost in Canada?

_writer_notes.focus: Sample monthly premiums. Based on: non-smoker, office worker, $5,000/month benefit, 90-day EP, to-age-65 benefit period.
key_points: Age 30, female: ~$90–$130/mo for $5,000/mo benefit
Age 35, male: ~$100–$150/mo for $5,000/mo benefit
Age 40, female: ~$140–$200/mo for $5,000/mo benefit
Age 45, male: ~$200–$280/mo for $5,000/mo benefit
Factors: age, sex, occupation class, health, smoker status, EP length, benefit period
_writer_notes.structure: Table with age bracket / male / female columns. Note: occupation class matters — manual workers pay more. CTA after table.
_writer_notes.callout_after: Blue callout: 'You can insure up to 85% of your pre-disability earned income. Most policies are designed to replace your net take-home pay, not your gross salary.'

page_data.heading: Group DI vs. Individual DI: What's the Difference?

_writer_notes.focus: Most Canadians have group DI through work but don't know how weak it is. Individual DI is portable, has better definitions, and often pays more.
key_points: Group DI: cheaper (employer shares cost), but: any-occ definition after 2 years, not portable, taxable if employer pays premiums, caps often too low
Individual DI: you own it, own-occ definition available, portable if you change jobs, benefits tax-free if you pay premiums
Best strategy: check what your group plan pays — if under 70% of income, consider topping up with individual DI
_writer_notes.structure: Comparison table: Group vs Individual on 6 dimensions.

_writer_notes.focus: Ontario-specific context: WSIB covers only workplace accidents — DI covers illness and non-workplace injuries too. FSRA regulates insurers.
key_points: WSIB (Workplace Safety and Insurance Board) only covers injuries that happen AT work
Most disabilities are caused by illness (cancer, mental health, heart disease) — WSIB won't cover these
Ontario has no provincial disability income program beyond CPP-D
CPP Disability pays a maximum of ~$1,583/month — far less than most incomes
FSRA-licensed Ontario advisors help you compare 10+ DI providers

faqs.question: Is disability insurance tax-deductible in Canada?
faqs.answer: No, personal disability insurance premiums are not tax-deductible. However, the benefits you receive are tax-free. If your employer pays DI premiums, the benefits are taxable income.
faqs._guidance: Clear answer. Explain the tax treatment both ways — personally paid vs employer-paid.

faqs.question: How long does disability insurance pay out?
faqs.answer: It depends on your benefit period. Short-term DI pays for 3–6 months. Long-term DI can pay for 2 years, 5 years, 10 years, or to age 65. Most people planning for serious disability choose a to-age-65 benefit period.
faqs._guidance: Explain options — don't just say 'it depends' without the specifics.

faqs.question: Can self-employed Canadians get disability insurance?
faqs.answer: Yes. Individual disability insurance is designed for the self-employed. You'll need to document your income (tax returns or financial statements) to prove your earned income. Most insurers require 2 years of filed returns.
faqs._guidance: This is a very common search. David example from _meta works here.

faqs.question: Does disability insurance cover mental health in Canada?
faqs.answer: Yes — modern disability insurance policies cover mental health conditions like depression, anxiety, and burnout if they prevent you from working. Mental health is now the leading cause of long-term disability claims in Canada.
faqs._guidance: Important — mental health is a growing concern. Be empathetic in tone.

faqs.question: What's the maximum disability insurance benefit in Canada?
faqs.answer: Most insurers cover up to 85% of your pre-disability earned income, combining group and individual plans. The maximum monthly benefit on most individual DI plans is $15,000–$20,000/month, though high-income earners can apply for specialty high-limit policies.
faqs._guidance: Give real numbers — most readers want to know the ceiling.

faqs.question: Will CPP disability replace my income?
faqs.answer: Partially. CPP Disability pays a flat benefit — in 2024, the maximum is approximately $1,583/month. For most Canadians earning above minimum wage, this doesn't come close to replacing your income. Personal DI fills the gap.
faqs._guidance: Use current year figure. Position CPP-D as supplement, not solution.
